title: Reading and Use of English - Part 5
description: You are going to read an article about storytelling and oral tradition. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Oldest Technology
content.subtitle: An article about the enduring power of oral storytelling.
content.text:
Before writing, before printing, before the internet — there was the voice. For most of human history, knowledge, memory, and meaning were carried not in books or databases but in stories told aloud by one person to another. [31] Oral tradition was not a primitive substitute for writing. It was a sophisticated technology in its own right, engineered over millennia to store and transmit the information a community needed to survive. [/31]

The mechanisms by which oral traditions preserve information are remarkable. Stories that must be remembered without writing tend to develop particular structural features: repetition, rhyme, rhythm, and formulaic phrases that act as memory anchors. The Homeric epics, for example, are riddled with repeated epithets — 'rosy-fingered dawn', 'swift-footed Achilles' — that would have served the oral poet as both mnemonic prompts and performance markers. [32] These repetitions, often criticised by modern readers as monotonous, were structural necessities in a world without written texts. [/32]

Anthropologists studying indigenous oral traditions have found that stories can carry accurate environmental data across hundreds of generations. Aboriginal Australian oral histories describing the flooding of coastal land have been cross-checked against geological records and found to correspond to events that occurred more than ten thousand years ago. [33] Far from being mythological embellishments, many elements of these stories encode precise geographical and ecological information that would have been vital to the communities who preserved them. [/33]

The arrival of literacy transformed but did not eliminate oral tradition. In cultures with widespread written literacy, storytelling shifted towards entertainment and artistic expression, losing some of its original mnemonic and practical functions. [34] What it retained, however, was arguably more important: the capacity to create shared emotional experience, to make abstract ideas tangible, and to build the sense of common identity that holds communities together. [/34] No written document has ever unified a room the way a well-told story can.

The neuroscience of storytelling offers some explanation for this power. When we listen to a narrative, our brains do not merely process information. They simulate experience: the same neural regions activated by real-life events fire in response to vivid stories. [35] This neural mirroring means that a story well told does not just inform; it creates a form of shared experience between teller and listener that is, neurologically speaking, distinct from any other form of communication. [/35]

In an age of podcasts, YouTube, and live streaming, it might seem that oral tradition has found its digital form. But storytelling researchers argue that something is lost when the voice is separated from the physical presence of both teller and listener. [36] The stories that change people — that shift their values or alter their understanding of themselves — tend to be told face to face, in rooms where something unrecordable passes between the speaker and the audience. [/36] The oldest technology, it seems, works best in the oldest conditions.

questions.question: In the opening paragraph, the writer describes oral tradition as
options: A) a less reliable method of storing information than writing.
B) a highly developed system for preserving and sharing community knowledge.
C) a practice that existed only in cultures without access to writing.
D) an early form of entertainment rather than a practical tool.
questions.explanation: The writer says oral tradition was 'a sophisticated technology in its own right, engineered over millennia to store and transmit the information a community needed to survive' — a highly developed system.

questions.question: The writer mentions the repeated epithets in Homeric epics to show that
options: A) ancient Greek poetry is of lower literary quality than modern readers expect.
B) oral poetry was less creative than written poetry because it relied on formulas.
C) features that seem like flaws served important functions in oral performance.
D) the Homeric epics were originally composed in writing and later performed aloud.
questions.explanation: The repetitions 'often criticised...as monotonous were structural necessities' — what seems like a flaw served an important mnemonic and performance function.

questions.question: The Aboriginal Australian oral histories are cited as evidence that
options: A) indigenous peoples possessed scientific knowledge superior to modern geology.
B) oral stories can preserve accurate factual information across thousands of years.
C) geology is a less reliable method of dating past events than oral tradition.
D) Aboriginal Australians experienced more significant environmental changes than other peoples.
questions.explanation: The stories 'correspond to events that occurred more than ten thousand years ago' and 'encode precise geographical and ecological information', proving oral stories can preserve accurate facts over millennia.

questions.question: According to the writer, when literacy became widespread, storytelling
options: A) became less important as a means of recording history.
B) changed in some of its functions but retained its power to create shared identity.
C) gradually disappeared as people relied more on written texts.
D) became more sophisticated as it incorporated ideas from written literature.
questions.explanation: Storytelling lost 'some of its original mnemonic and practical functions' but 'retained...the capacity to create shared emotional experience' and 'common identity'.

questions.question: The neuroscience research described in the fifth paragraph shows that listening to stories
options: A) is more mentally demanding than any other communication activity.
B) activates the same brain regions as actually experiencing the events described.
C) improves memory and concentration in ways that other activities cannot.
D) creates stronger emotional responses than reading the same stories silently.
questions.explanation: The text says 'the same neural regions activated by real-life events fire in response to vivid stories', meaning the brain treats stories similarly to actual experiences.

questions.question: In the final paragraph, the writer suggests that digital storytelling
options: A) is more effective than live storytelling for reaching large audiences.
B) has entirely replaced the traditions of face-to-face oral storytelling.
C) lacks something essential that is present in physical, in-person storytelling.
D) will eventually develop tools that can replicate live storytelling experiences.
questions.explanation: The writer says 'something is lost when the voice is separated from the physical presence' and that the most powerful stories are 'told face to face', suggesting digital formats lack a vital quality.
